post:
I've been thinking about something George Hincapie said to me yesterday that completely changed how I see Paris-Roubaix.

We were talking about positioning into the cobble sectors, and I asked him about the wattage difference between being in the top 10 versus the back of the bunch. I expected him to give me some crazy numbers about the slingshot effect and accordion dynamics.

Instead, he said: "It's not even the watts. It's the danger, the risk. If there's a crash in 20th wheel, the whole peloton's blocked."

That hit me. Here I was thinking like a typical cycling nerd, obsessing over power numbers, when the real game is about risk management. It's not about how many watts you can push - it's about not getting caught behind the inevitable chaos.

This is classic Hincapie wisdom. Seven times at Roubaix teaches you what really matters. The guy who won stages at the Tour de France and podiumed at Roubaix isn't thinking about marginal gains. He's thinking about staying upright and keeping his wheels turning.

It reminded me of something I learned the hard way in my racing days. I used to obsess over being in the perfect aerodynamic position, calculating wind angles and draft benefits. Then one day I got caught behind a crash because I was too focused on the technical stuff instead of reading the race situation.

The lesson applies way beyond the cobbles of northern France. Whether you're in your local crit or just trying to survive the Saturday morning chaingang, positioning is about more than going fast. It's about staying safe and staying in the game.

Hincapie also thinks Vanderpoel has the advantage over Pogačar this Sunday. Not because he's stronger, but because Alpecin has better team support for this specific race. Their flat powerhouses can stay with Mathieu deeper into the race than UAE's climber-heavy roster can support Tadej.

Sometimes the best tactical advice is the simplest: don't get caught behind the crash.

Full conversation with George is live now - one of my favourite episodes this year.
angle: Personal reflection on risk management vs power obsession in racing positioning
